questions.stem: A nurse caring for a ventilated patient wants to implement the VAP prevention bundle. Which combination of interventions is CORRECT according to current evidence?

options.text: HOB flat, scheduled oral care q8h with water, continuous sedation infusion
HOB 30–45°, oral care with 0.12% chlorhexidine q6h, daily sedation vacation with SBT
HOB 30–45°, oral care q12h with water, sedation vacation only when FiO2 <0.40
HOB 15°, chlorhexidine oral care q8h, continuous sedation to prevent self-extubation

questions.rationale: The VAP prevention bundle includes: (1) HOB elevation 30–45° to reduce aspiration of gastric contents; (2) oral decontamination with 0.12% chlorhexidine gluconate every 6 hours to reduce oropharyngeal bacterial burden; (3) daily sedation interruption (sedation vacation) — allows neurological assessment and reduces days on ventilator; (4) daily spontaneous breathing trials (SBT) to assess readiness for extubation — the most effective strategy for reducing VAP is minimizing ventilator days.

distractorRationales.a: HOB flat, scheduled oral care q8h with water, continuous sedation infusion is not the best answer, without accounting for The VAP prevention bundle includes: (1) HOB elevation 30–45° to reduce aspiration of gastric contents. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.
distractorRationales.c: HOB 30–45°, oral care q12h with water, sedation vacation only when FiO2 <0.40 fits a lower-risk interpretation, although the nurse should be guided by (4) daily spontaneous breathing trials (SBT) to assess readiness for extubation — the most effective strategy for reducing. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The safer answer is HOB 30–45°, oral care with 0.12% chlorhexidine q6h, daily sedation vacation with SBT because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes HOB 15°, chlorhexidine oral care q8h, continuous sedation to prevent self-extubation the focus, and misses that (3) daily sedation interruption (sedation vacation) — allows neurological assessment and reduces days on ventilator. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so HOB 30–45°, oral care with 0.12% chlorhexidine q6h, daily sedation vacation with SBT is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: VAP bundle: HOB 30–45°, chlorhexidine oral care, daily sedation vacation + SBT. Each element is independently evidence-based.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: Age >65 and female gender fits a lower-risk interpretation, instead of responding to Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of medication toxicity and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: This option overweights Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29, instead of responding to Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The priority is History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic, not a step that delays stabilization of medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so The priority is History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic, not a step that delays stabilization of medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: The problem with Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, rather than acting on Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. The safer answer is Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, when the bedside priority is driven by UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching. The correct path is Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care as the key issue, despite the clinical warning in Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.

questions.stem: A patient delivers vaginally and 30 minutes later has an estimated blood loss of 750 mL with a soft, boggy uterus displaced to the right. BP is 94/58, HR 118. Which sequence of interventions is correct?

options.text: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider
Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement
Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion
Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. (2) Empty the bladder — a full bladder displaces the uterus and prevents contraction (uterus displaced to the right = full bladder). (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. (4) Notify provider if no response. Bladder emptying is critical and often overlooked — the displaced, boggy uterus signals bladder distension. Epinephrine (D) and hysterectomy are not indicated at this stage.

distractorRationales.a: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider is not the best answer, but the stem cue is BP is 94/58, HR 118. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for bleeding-risk intervention while bleeding or anticoagulation injury progresses.
distractorRationales.c: Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion is not the best answer, even though the higher-risk finding is BP is 94/58, HR 118. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for bleeding-risk intervention while bleeding or anticoagulation injury progresses.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent, although the nurse should be guided by (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so The safer answer is Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement because delaying bleeding-risk intervention can worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus displaced to the right after delivery = full bladder; empty bladder first, then fundal massage + oxytocin.

questions.stem: A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth, and swelling. Vital signs are stable. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses
Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result
Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound
Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: Unilateral calf pain, warmth, and swelling in a post-operative patient are classic signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can be ordered promptly. Homan's sign (B) is unreliable and has been removed from clinical guidelines. Warmth and elevation (A) delay diagnosis. Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses fits a lower-risk interpretation, instead of responding to Unilateral calf pain, warmth. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound; otherwise circulation or perfusion remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, but the stem cue is Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate. Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring, but the stem cue is The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound is safer because it addresses circulation or perfusion before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Post-op calf pain + swelling = suspect DVT; notify provider and anticipate duplex ultrasound — Homan's sign is unreliable.

questions.stem: A client with depression says: 'I don't see the point in trying anymore. Nothing I do matters.' Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'
'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'
'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'
'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'

questions.rationale: Option B is an open-ended exploration that invites the client to elaborate, demonstrating active listening without judgment. It also screens for suicidal ideation — 'nothing I do matters' and 'no point in trying' may signal passive suicidal thinking. Option A uses false reassurance ('so much to live for') and assumes understanding without exploring further. Option C minimizes the client's experience ('everyone feels that way'). Option D is non-therapeutic confrontation that dismisses the client's feelings.

distractorRationales.a: The problem with 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.' is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, instead of responding to therapeutic communication safety. Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with 'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.' is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, although the nurse should be guided by therapeutic communication safety. The nurse should prioritize 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'; otherwise clinical safety risk remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward 'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.', without accounting for therapeutic communication safety. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.' targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication: use open-ended statements to explore meaning, especially when statements may signal suicidal ideation.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: This answer choice treats The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television as the key issue, instead of responding to The SpO2 is 91%. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The priority is Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: The clinical error in Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, when the bedside priority is driven by work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95% is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min would not address the priority, when the bedside priority is driven by Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95% targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

visualRationale.caption: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
visualRationale.conclusion: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing vital signs in the pediatric unit. Which set of vital signs is within normal limits for a healthy 3-year-old toddler?
questions.rationale: Normal vital signs for a 3-year-old toddler: HR 80–130 bpm, RR 22–37 breaths/min, BP approximately 90–100/55–65 mmHg. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 are all within expected ranges. Choice A has a bradycardic HR and adult-level BP. Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress, and RR 40 is elevated. Choice D has significant bradycardia. Key developmental milestone: vital signs normalize toward adult ranges with age — neonates have the highest HR and RR, lowest BP.

distractorRationales.a: Selecting HR 68, RR 14, BP 120/80 would be unsafe, instead of responding to Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56; otherwise oxygenation or ventilation remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: Selecting HR 145, RR 40, BP 80/48 would be unsafe, rather than acting on Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in HR 55, RR 18, BP 110/72 is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by RR 40 is elevated. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy at 8:00 AM. Per current ASA NPO guidelines, what are the correct restrictions for solid food and clear liquids?

options.text: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid
Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery
Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, clear juice, black coffee) allowed until 2 hours before
No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk

questions.rationale: Current ASA NPO guidelines (2017, updated 2023): Solid foods, fried/fatty meals: NPO ≥8 hours. Light meals (toast, clear liquids): NPO ≥6 hours. Non-clear liquids including milk and formula: NPO ≥6 hours. Clear liquids (water, apple juice without pulp, clear carbonated beverages, black coffee or tea without cream, sports drinks): may be consumed up to 2 hours before elective procedures. Chewing gum and hard candy: treat as clear liquid — stop 2 hours before. These evidence-based guidelines replaced the outdated 'NPO after midnight' rule. The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance, and perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction.

distractorRationales.a: The problem with Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, despite the clinical warning in ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before. The safer answer is Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, because delaying glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization can worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.b: Selecting Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery would be unsafe, despite the clinical warning in The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water,; otherwise unstable glucose physiology remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk fits a lower-risk interpretation, while the decisive cue is ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water,, not a step that delays stabilization of unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before; clear liquids until 2 hrs before. 'NPO after midnight' is outdated and harmful. Clear liquids: water, clear juice (no pulp), black coffee/tea.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type 1 diabetes before breakfast. Which action by the nurse requires IMMEDIATE correction?

options.text: Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe
Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration
Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal
Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. It should NEVER be mixed with any other insulin, including rapid-acting insulins. Mixing glargine with other insulins alters its pH, causing it to crystallize and unpredictably changes both insulins' absorption profiles. The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Each insulin must be drawn in a separate syringe and administered as separate injections. Drawing up separately is correct. Injecting aspart before meals (pre-prandial) is appropriate. Site rotation is correct practice.

distractorRationales.a: This option overweights Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe, instead of responding to It has its own pH and formulation. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration; otherwise unstable glucose physiology remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal, even though the higher-risk finding is Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization while unstable glucose physiology progresses.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh as the key issue, although the nurse should be guided by The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration; otherwise unstable glucose physiology remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: Glargine (Lantus) = NEVER MIX with other insulins. It has its own pH and formulation. Mix-up can cause crystal formation and unpredictable glycemic effect. Use separate syringes, separate injections. Applies also to detemir (Levemir).
